Plaintiff Eduardo Torres Request For A Prove-Up Hearing In Support Of Default Judgment Against Defendant
Off Calendar Aug-06-2019 Off Calendar

Defendant Bay View Painting'S Motion For Reconsideration (Per Ex Parte Order Filed On 7-3-19)
Off Calendar Jul-29-2019 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: DEFENDANT BAY VIEW PAINTING'S MOTION TO RECONSIDER MOTION FOR ORDER VACATING AND SETTING ASIDE ENTRY OF DEFAULT

No appearances. The Court adopts its tentative ruling as follows: Defendant Bay View Painting's motion for reconsideration is granted. The Court exercises its inherent powers to reconsider its prior order. (See Le Francois v. Goel (2005) 35 Cal.4th 1094.) Upon further reconsideration, the Court grants Defendant's motion to vacate the default entered on August 21, 2018.

Code of Civil Procedure 425.11(c) provides that "the plaintiff shall serve the statement [of damages] on the defendant before a default may be taken." In this case, plaintiff entered default on August 21, 2018 and filed the statement of damages on April 23, 2019. The statement of damages indicates that it was served on July 9, 2018, which was prior to entry of the default. However, the evidence indicates that Plaintiff may have served the statement of damages at the wrong address.

Plaintiff served the summons and complaint on Defendant's owner at 466 Milton Avenue, San Bruno and obtained a notice and acknowledgment of receipt. (Massey Affidavit, Ex. B.) In addition, Plaintiff's counsel was told that 466 Milton was Defendant's correct address. (Amended Declaration of David Chen Esq. in Support of Plaintiff Eduardo Torres' Prove-Up, 7.) However, Plaintiff served the statement of damages at 464 Milton Avenue, and contrary to the proof of service in the document itself, there is no signed notice and acknowledgment of receipt. (Massey Affidavit, Ex. D.) Although Plaintiff's counsel declares that his research showed the latter address to be correct, he does not explain why he did not serve the statement of damages at the first, correct address. Given the liberal policy in favor of hearing cases on the merits, the Court concludes that the default should be set aside. (See Fasuyi v. Permatex, Inc. (2008) 167 Cal.App.4th 681, 692-694 [abuse of discretion for trial court to deny motion to set aside default and default judgment where there was conflicting evidence as to whether statement of damages had been properly served].)

Plaintiff's failure to serve the statement of damages prior to entry of the default was clear on the record given the address discrepancy. Accordingly, the default was void and can be set aside at any time. (See Hamm v. Elkin (1987) 196 Cal.App.3d 1343 [statement of damages must be served before entry of default]; Plotitsa v. Superior Court (1983) 140 Cal.App.3d 755, 760 ["[A] default entered without any such statement being served on the defendant is void on the face of the record and may be successfully challenged beyond the six-month limitation period specified in section 473."].) Although Defendant did not (and has not) sought to set aside the default on that ground, the Court nevertheless concludes that relief is appropriate. Plaintiff's counsel shall pay Defendant $1,000.00 to reimburse it for the costs it incurred in opposing Plaintiff's ill-founded motion. (Code Civ. Proc. 473(c)(1)(A).) Prevailing party to submit a proposed form of order. Judge: Ethan P. Schulman; Clerk: E. Larnauti; Not reported (302/EPS)

ORDER TO SHOW CAUSE
Off Calendar Jul-11-2019 Continued To Sep-17-19 At 10:30 A.M. In Dept 610

Plaintiff Eduardo Torres Request For A Prove-Up Hearing In Support Of Default Judgment Against Defendant
Off Calendar Jul-03-2019 Continued To Aug-06-19 At 09:00 A.M. In Dept 514

Notice Of Motion And Motion For Order Vacating And Setting Aside Entry Of Default Against Defendant Bay View Painting
Off Calendar Jul-01-2019 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:

APPEARANCES: Atty. DAVID W. CHEN, ESQ. of Law Office of David W. Chen (Tel: 510-575-0851) appearing for the plaintiff;
Atty. MELANIE D. MASSEY, ESQ. of Clapp Moroney Vucinich Beeman Scheley (Tel: 650-989-5400) appearing for defendant Bay View Painting.

DEFENDANT BAY VIEW PAINTING'S Motion For Order Vacating And Setting Aside Entry Of Default Against Defendant Bay View Painting.

The matter is argued and submitted by counsel for parties.

The Court adopts its tentative ruling as follows: Defendant Bay View Painting's motion for order vacating and setting aside default is denied. Mandatory relief is not available under the attorney affidavit of fault provision of Code of Civil Procedure Section 473(b) because Defendant has not submitted "an attorney's sworn affidavit attesting to his or her mistake, inadvertence, surprise, or neglect." (Code Civ. Proc. Sec. 473(b).) Defendant does not contend that its default was taken as a result of the mistake, inadvertence, surprise, or neglect of counsel, but rather "due to his insurance broker's mistaken belief that the matter was a workers' compensation issue." (Mot. at 3; Massey Decl. 6, 7.) The declaration accompanying the motion establishes that Defendant's current counsel was not retained to represent the Defendant in this matter until May 23, 2019, long after Defendant's default was taken on August 21, 2018, and its first mention of any involvement by counsel is a vague reference to a conversation that took place some time "in 2019" between an attorney for Defendant and the insurance broker during which the attorney corrected the broker's misunderstanding. (Mot. at 3; Massey Decl. 12.) The Court finds that "the default or dismissal was not in fact caused by the attorney's mistake, inadvertence, surprise, or neglect." (Code Civ. Proc. Sec. 473(b).)

In addition, Defendant's motion for mandatory relief and its alternative motion for discretionary relief under Section 473(b) are both untimely, as they were not filed within 6 months of the clerk's entry of default on August 21, 2018. (Code Civ. Proc. Sec. 473(b) [application for relief "shall be made within a reasonable time, in no case exceeding six months, after the judgment, dismissal, order, or proceeding was taken."].) "The six-month limit is mandatory; a court has no authority to grant relief under section 473, subdivision (b), unless an application is made within the six-month period." (Arumbula v. Union Carbide Corp. (2005) 128 Cal.App.4th 333, 340.) The Court has no authority to grant relief from the six-month limit, whether based on mistake, inadvertence, surprise, or excusable or an attorney's affidavit of fault. (Id. at 345; accord, Austin v. Los Angeles Unified School Dist. (2016) 244 Cal.App.4th 918, 918 ["The six-month time limitation is jurisdictional; the court has no power to grant relief under section 473 once the time has lapsed."].) (Cf. Fasuyi v. Permatex, Inc. (2008) 167 Cal.App.4th 681, 687-689 [request for entry of default filed February 14, 2007; default judgment entered February 28, 2007; motion for relief from default filed March 23, 2007].)

The Court signs a formal order.

JUDGE: ETHAN P. SCHULMAN; CLERK: SEAN KANE; COURT REPORTER: ANTHONY VAUGHN, CSR #6185, TONYVAUGHN@YAHOO.COM (302/EPS)
